text:
Sure, I'd be happy to help you plan your trip to Copenhagen. Here are some interesting spots you should consider visiting:

Location 1:
Location: Amarienborg
Reason: It's the home of the Danish royal family. Plus, you can witness the change of guard at 12:00.
Price: $1.25 (requires reservation)
Opening days: All week

Location 2:
Location: Frederiks Kirke
Reason: It's a famous modern architecture.
Price: $20
Opening days: From Tuesday to Friday

Location 3:
Location: The Little Mermaid Statue
Reason: It's the character from Grimm's Fairy Tales.
Price: Free
Opening days: All week

Location 4:
Location: Noma
Reason: It's a world-famous restaurant that offers a culinary masterpiece.
Price: $100
Opening days: All week

Location 5:
Location: Rundetaarn
Reason: It's an Astronomy tower from the 17th century.
Price: DKK 40
Opening days: All week

Location 6:
Location: Christiansborg Palace
Reason: It's one of the most significant landmarks in Copenhagen, both historically and politically.
Price: Free
Opening days: Tuesday to Sunday

And here are some travel tips for your Copenhagen trip:

Tips:

The type of power sockets used in Denmark are Type A and Type K.
The official currency in Denmark is the Danish Krone (DKK). Most places do not accept euros.
There are in total 6 metros in Copenhagen. Visitors can reach anywhere by metro.
The weather in Copenhagen in December is below -15 degrees Celsius.
The official language is Danish, but English is widely spoken in Copenhagen.
Change your memory. The “hello” in Danish is “hej”, “thank you” in Danish is “farvel”, “goodbye” in Danish is “tak”.
I hope this helps with your planning!

National Museum of Denmark

National Museum of Denmark
It's the country’s largest cultural history museum, offering an extensive collection of artifacts and exhibits that showcase Denmark’s rich history.

Skagens Museum
Skagens Museum
Skagens Museum
It's a renowned art museum that showcases the works of the Skagen Painters, a group of Scandinavian artists who settled in Skagen during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
